text: In this video, we demonstrate a novel robotically-assisted technique for a total pancreatectomy with autologous islet transplantation in which the pancreas is resected in its entirety.
The robotic and laparoscopic ports are positioned, along with the camera port, while the liver is retracted to provide a clear view of the surgical field.
Laparoscopic instruments are used to mobilize the right colon down to the terminal ileum as part of the initial steps in the total pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs a Kocher maneuver to mobilize the transverse duodenum from the ligament of Treitz, working from the right side of the table beneath the mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon creates an opening into the foramen of Winslow, providing access to the posterior aspect of the pancreas during the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon pulls the jejunum into the right upper quadrant, positioning it beneath the mesenteric vessels during the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon transects the jejunum using a 3.5 mm linear cutting stapler, approximately ten centimeters from the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon dissects the inferior pancreatic border towards the tail, visible at the top of the screen, while simultaneously taking down the splenic flexure.
The da Vinci robotic system is positioned and docked at the head of the surgical table, ready for use in the remaining steps of the pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to divide the tissue overlying the porta hepatis, exposing the superior border of the pancreas and the common hepatic artery.
The surgeon isolates the splenic artery using a vessel loop, clearly delineating the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clears surrounding tissue from the gastroduodenal artery, revealing its structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the splenic artery with a vessel loop, completing the isolation process initiated in previous steps.
The surgeon identifies the gastric artery, visible within the surgical field, using the da Vinci robotic system.
The surgeon clips the identified vessel twice proximally and once distally, then divides the vessel between the clips.
The surgeon creates a tunnel behind the duodenum, carefully maneuvering through the surrounding tissue to establish a clear passage.
The surgeon divides the duodenum using a 3.8mm linear cutting stapler, completing the separation of this section from adjacent structures.
The surgeon develops the retropancreatic space, creating a visible separation between the pancreas and underlying structures.
The camera angle provides enhanced visualization of the retropancreatic tunnel, with the surgeon viewing the tunnel from a caudal to cephalid direction.
The surgeon isolates the common bile duct using a vessel loop, clearly delineating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully examines the surgical field to identify any aberrant right hepatic arterial anomalies, ensuring precise navigation around these structures.
The surgeon transects the duct using a linear stapler, effectively sealing it to prevent bile spillage.
The surgeon isolates the retropancreatic tunnel over the portal vein using a moistened umbilical tape, enhancing the visibility and separation of the anatomical structures in this area.
The surgeon isolates the splenic vein and encircles it with a vessel loop, ensuring clear visualization of the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates the gastroepiploic vein and transects it, completing this step in the pancreatectomy procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the pancreas from the lateral border of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein, working in a caudate to cephalid direction to gradually separate the uncinate process from the adjacent vessels.
The surgeon ligates incident venous branches, ensuring secure closure and preventing blood flow through these vessels.
The surgeon reflects the SMV portal vein medially, revealing the SMA posteriorly on the right side of the screen.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the inferior pancreatic duodenal artery, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places clips on the artery and ligates it using a ligature divider, securing the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the superior pancreatic duodenal vein, dissects it from surrounding tissue, and divides it using a surgical stapler.
The surgeon lifts the entire pancreas from the retroperitoneum, revealing that it remains attached only by the gastroduodenal artery, splenic artery, and splenic vein.
The surgeon transects the splenic artery, gastroduodenal artery, and splenic vein sequentially using vascular load staplers, completing the separation of the pancreas from these vessels.
This minimized warm ischemic time to less than a minute.
The surgeon extracts the entire pancreatic specimen, which has been fully detached from surrounding vascular structures.
The surgeon prepares the pancreatic islets while simultaneously performing reconstruction of the surgical site.
The surgeon reinforces the inferior pancreatic duodenal artery with five O-proline sutures, ensuring secure attachment and stability of the vessel.
The surgeon brings the jejunum through a defect in the right mesocolon, positioning it for further reconstruction.
The surgeon performs an end-to-side hepaticojejunostomy, using a continuous suture technique to connect the hepatic duct to the jejunum.
A hop stent is placed in the bile duct, followed by the surgeon proceeding with the anterior row of the hepaticojejunostomy.
The surgeon performs a Hofmeister-type hand-sewn end-to-side gastrojejunostomy, constructing the anastomosis in two distinct layers.
The surgeon partially excises the staple line at the splenic vein stump and manipulates a fourteen gauge angiocath into the portal vein.
The robotic arm holds the catheter in a fixed position, maintaining its stability within the surgical field.
The surgeon connects sterile tubing to the catheter, ensuring a secure attachment.
The surgeon infuses islets into the portal vein, completing the transplantation process.
The surgeon closes the splenic vein stump using IV-O Surgipro sutures, securing the vessel with precise stitching.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamp from the portal vein, which was previously manipulated with a fourteen gauge angiocath.
The surgeon applies a staple near the portal vein to reinforce the area, ensuring secure closure and stability.
